summary: Spiritwood, North Dakota, was where my great-grandmother Nora Johnson lived and where the original Johnson farm was located. The farm stayed in the family until it was sold in 2016. It’s also the place where the antique Singer sewing machine originated, a treasured family heirloom that connected me to my ancestors.

Nora Johnson was my great-grandmother and a remarkable woman who was a teacher before she married into the Johnson family. She was very talented, especially in sewing, and used a push pedal Singer sewing machine to make clothes for her family. Her sewing machine and the bits she kept in its drawers have become a treasured connection to my family’s past.

Minnesota is where my great-grandfather’s family first settled after emigrating from Sweden, specifically in Litchfield. It’s also where my mother lives now, and where the Singer sewing machine was sent to keep it in the family. Minnesota is part of the larger region connected to my family’s history, including the large family photo from the late 1800s.

Wisconsin was part of the region where my great-grandmother’s family lived, as shown in a late 1800s family photo that included nineteen children and their parents. It was mentioned alongside Minnesota as the area with one of the largest families at the time. Though I didn’t have direct ties to Wisconsin, it was part of the broader family history I learned about.

Oscar Johnson was my great-grandfather, whose father made the decision to emigrate from Sweden to the United States. Oscar’s family settled first in Litchfield, Minnesota, and then moved to Spiritwood, North Dakota. He was part of a farming family that passed down their trade and land through generations.

Sweden was the country my great-grandfather Oscar Johnson’s father and his brother emigrated from in the late 1800s. Leaving Sweden, they settled in the United States, first in Minnesota and then North Dakota. This move was the beginning of our family’s American story and farming legacy.

Litchfield, Minnesota, was the first place my great-grandfather’s family settled after emigrating from Sweden. It was an important stop before they eventually moved to Spiritwood, North Dakota. This town marks the early chapter of our family’s life in America and their farming roots.

The Singer Corporation made the antique sewing machine that belonged to my great-grandmother Nora Johnson. This push pedal Singer sewing machine was her tool for making clothes for her family, and it became a cherished family heirloom passed down through generations. The machine connected me deeply to my family’s history and traditions.
